advantages: Regression: directly optimizes numeric error (MAE, RMSE) when the business needs a quantity — price, demand, latency — not just a category.
Regression: preserves magnitude and ordering in predictions; a $50k error is treated as larger than a $5k error under squared loss.
Classification: matches discrete decision workflows — approve/deny, route to team A/B, block/allow — where only the class matters at action time.
Classification: supports calibrated probabilities for risk ranking and threshold tuning without pretending classes are equally spaced numbers.
Classification: standard metrics (precision, recall, F1, AUC) address class imbalance and asymmetric error costs better than rounding regression outputs.
disadvantages: Regression: inappropriate for unordered categories — predicting digit 4.7 or treating class IDs as a number line misrepresents the target.
Regression: sensitive to outliers under MSE; a few extreme y values can dominate training when the deployment need was a robust category decision.
Classification: discards fine-grained numeric detail when bins or hard labels replace a continuous quantity the user actually needs.
Classification: accuracy alone can mislead on imbalanced data; rare-class recall may be poor even at high accuracy.
Classification: threshold choice is an extra deployment decision — the same probability model behaves differently at 0.3 vs 0.7 cutoffs.
